title: Microservices Principles & DDD
learningObjectives: Tell **strategic DDD** (where to draw lines between areas of the business) apart from **tactical DDD** (how to code aggregates, events, and value objects) and link both to service boundaries
Explain why **one database per service** (as a goal) and **clear APIs or events between teams** help you avoid a **distributed monolith** that is still impossible to release independently

Describe the **aggregate** as a safe place for one database transaction, and choose **sync calls** vs **async messages** based on user wait time, downtime risk, and how much delay the business accepts
Use plain-language ideas behind **anti-corruption layer**, **published language**, and **customer–supplier** when talking about integrations with legacy or vendor systems
Map ideas to Java/Spring in simple terms: keep domains in separate modules, do not share JPA entities across areas, and use an **outbox** so “save order” and “publish event” succeed or fail together
Tell interview stories with **one concrete failure** (duplicates, rounding mismatch, shared DB pain) and **one clear fix you can measure** (dedup table, single owner for tax rules, migration plan)

sections.title: Why this topic shows up in real interviews
sections.content:
People often sell microservices as “scale.” In real systems, they change how teams **own data** and how **money and orders stay correct** when the network is slow or a service is down. If you split services but still use **one shared database**, share JPA entities across “areas,” or chain many **blocking HTTP calls** for a single button, you do not get independence. You get a **distributed monolith**: releases still block each other, but failures are **partial**, retries can **double-charge** or **double-ship**, and on-call needs many tools to see the full story.

Interviewers usually do not want book quotes. They want to hear **where you draw lines**, **who owns which rules**, and **what happens when a call fails or runs twice**. Good answers use simple words for hard topics: same word (“total,” “account”) can mean different things in billing vs CRM; **aggregates** match “what must stay consistent in one commit”; **context map** means “how teams depend on each other.”

A strong answer often has four simple parts: (1) name the **business area** and the words experts use for it, (2) say what must stay **consistent in one transaction** vs what can catch up later, (3) give **one real failure** from messy boundaries (duplicates, rounding fights, migration fights), (4) give a **fix** people can operate (dedup keys, outbox, one owner for tax rules, module rules in the build).

At company scale, this matters because “more services” means **more choices about blast radius**. True independent deploys need **clear data ownership**, **clear SLOs**, and teams that match the architecture. DDD is a shared language for talking with product: what is **core** to the business, what is **supporting**, which team leads a model, and where **eventual consistency** is acceptable if you say it out loud.

Senior answers sound operational in plain English: **timeouts** so threads do not hang forever, **idempotency** so retries are safe, **at-least-once messaging** so consumers must handle duplicates, **projection lag** so search is slightly behind writes, and **tracing** so async bugs are visible — not “clean code” alone.

sections.title: Microservices Principles & DDD — theory that sticks
sections.content:
### Day 51 — Microservices and DDD in simple terms

This day links two ideas: **why** we split systems (team ownership, different scale needs, fault isolation) and **how** we model the business so services are not just “remote function calls over HTTP.”

---

### 1. What is DDD in one interview sentence?
**Domain-Driven Design** means building software around a **clear business model**. Experts and developers share the **same words** (**ubiquitous language**). The model is split into **bounded contexts** (areas where words and rules stay consistent). Inside a context you use **tactical patterns**: **entities**, **value objects**, **aggregates**, **repositories**, **domain events**, and sometimes **domain services** — all to keep **business rules** true.

**Microservices** means separate deployable services, usually **one team owns one service**, talking over the network. **Important:** DDD does **not** require microservices. Microservices do **not** require DDD. They work well together when **service boundaries** match **real business boundaries** and **real team ownership**.

---

### 2. Bounded context — the real line between “modules”
A **bounded context** is the smallest place where the model **fits together**: one meaning per term, rules hold, and refactors do not surprise other teams. The word **Account** can mean one thing in CRM and another in the ledger — that is **two models**, not sloppy naming. Between contexts you **translate** data and language.

Common relationship names (for interviews): **anti-corruption layer** (translate messy external data into your model), **published language** (a stable shared format), **open host service** (upstream offers a clear integration API), **conformist** (downstream accepts upstream’s model), **customer–supplier** (downstream can negotiate priorities with upstream).

**Interview angle:** If you cannot say “this rule must always be true here” and “across the line we may be slightly behind,” you only renamed packages — you do not have a real boundary.

---

### 3. Aggregates — keep one database transaction focused
An **aggregate** is a small cluster of objects with one **aggregate root** as the front door. Outside code talks to the root, not to hidden inner objects, so **rules cannot be bypassed**. Other aggregates should reference each other by **id**, not by loaded object graphs, to avoid huge loads, accidental cross-service joins, and fake “distributed transactions.”

Usually **one transaction changes one aggregate**. If a business process spans aggregates, you **finish one commit**, then use **events** and **async steps**, or a **saga** (a multi-step flow with undo steps). Trying to “make one HTTP chain atomic” across many services is a common source of production pain.

---

### 4. Microservice ideas — what the network changes
Simple principles people expect: **one main business reason to change per service**, **each service owns its data** (goal: separate database), **failures should not spread** (timeouts, circuit breakers, bulkheads), **good logs and traces**, and **shared standards** (auth, tracing IDs) without one team controlling every detail.

**Cost to name honestly:** more latency, more ops work, **eventual consistency** in many flows, and you must design **retries** and **duplicate handling** on purpose.

| Idea | Healthy sign | Bad smell |
|------|--------------|----------|
| Team owns service | Team can deploy on its own | Every change needs a meeting with three teams |
| Loose coupling | Translate at edges with APIs/events | Share JPA entities between services |
| Cohesive service | Features belong together | “Tiny service for one helper table” |
| Resilience | Timeouts, safe retries, dead-letter queues | Endless retries on non-idempotent work |
| Observable | Traces across sync and async | “We cannot tell why loyalty doubled” |

---

### 5. Interview questions you should expect (plain versions)
1. **Where do you cut services?** By **business capability** and **team ownership**, not by “one table = one service.”
2. **How do you keep data consistent?** **Sync** when the user must see the result now (with timeouts). **Async + eventual** for many cross-team updates, with **outbox** and **idempotent** consumers.
3. **What is an anti-corruption layer?** A **translator** at the edge so legacy or vendor models do not leak into your core names and rules.
4. **Aggregates vs transactions?** **One aggregate per commit** is the default; **sagas** for long flows; avoid pretending six REST calls are one database transaction.

**Interview angle:** If you say Kafka, also say **messages can arrive more than once** — so the consumer must **deduplicate** or use **unique keys** in the database.

---

### 6. Java / Spring — how this looks in real code (high level)
- Start with a **modular monolith**: separate Maven/Gradle modules per context; **ArchUnit** can fail the build if `orders` imports `catalog` domain classes.
- **Ports and adapters:** domain defines `InventoryPort`; infrastructure implements it with `WebClient` and timeouts.
- Do not return or share **`@Entity`** across service boundaries — use **DTOs** and map in one place.
- **Transactional outbox:** in the **same database transaction** as saving the order, insert an **outbox row**; a small publisher process sends to Kafka (or poll another process). That avoids “saved in DB but message never sent” or “message sent twice.”
- **Tests:** domain tests without Spring; **contract tests** (e.g. Pact) at boundaries when two teams disagree.

---

### 7. Strategic vs tactical — do not mix them up
| Level | Plain question | Typical output |
|-------|----------------|----------------|
| Strategic | Where are the business areas? How do teams relate? | Context map, workshops |
| Tactical | How do we code rules cleanly inside one area? | Aggregates, events, repositories |

A common trap: pretty **tactical** code inside one giant service with **no strategic** lines — you still have a big ball of mud, just with nicer class names.

---

### 8. Sixty-second story (simple wording)
**What users saw:** finance said order totals and invoices differed by **pennies**. **What went wrong:** **Orders** and **Billing** both calculated **tax** with slightly different rounding. Each team thought they were right. **Fix:** pick **one owner** for tax rules (often billing/pricing), version the policy, and store **frozen prices** on the order when the customer pays. **Prevention:** a small **glossary** for money fields, **contract tests** on totals, and a dashboard for mismatches. **Takeaway:** splitting services does not fix unclear business language — **talking and agreeing** does.

---

### 9. Conway’s Law — short and practical
**Conway’s Law:** your system shape tends to match how people talk at work. If teams are unclear, the code will be unclear too. **Platform teams** help most when they give **easy patterns** (tracing, auth, outbox helpers), not when they force “microservices everywhere” on day one.

sections.title: Microservices Principles & DDD — basic
sections.description: Plain Java reference output aligned with REST/API concepts for this day.
sections.output:
=== When separate services often make sense ===
Situation                         | Plain explanation
Read-heavy search vs writes       | Scale or deploy search differently
Different release schedules       | One team ships often, one ships rarely
Compliance / card data            | Isolate sensitive parts
Many teams, clear ownership       | Matches who maintains what

=== When one well-structured app is often better ===
Situation                         | Plain explanation
Small team, early product         | Extra services add ops work
Boundaries still unclear          | Draw lines before splitting
Heavy shared rules across areas   | Model first, then split data
Low traffic internal tool         | Network calls add little value

=== DDD building blocks (simple meanings) ===
Block              | Plain meaning
Entity             | Thing with identity that can change over time
Value object       | Small immutable piece (e.g. money with currency)
Aggregate root     | The one door to change a cluster safely
Domain event       | Something that already happened (past tense)
Repository         | Load/save aggregates; hides the database
Domain service     | Rule that does not fit one entity
Factory            | Creates a new aggregate with valid start state
ACL                | Translator for external or legacy data shapes

=== How areas relate (names interviewers like) ===
Partnership        | Two teams share and evolve one model
Customer-Supplier  | Downstream asks upstream for roadmap help
Conformist         | Downstream copies upstream model as-is
Anti-corruption    | Downstream translates foreign model
Open Host          | Upstream publishes a clear integration API
Published Language | Shared stable format between areas

sections.title: Microservices Principles & DDD — intermediate
sections.description: Scenarios and simulations mirroring production API behavior.
sections.output:
=== Order context simulation (aggregate + port) ===

--- Scenario 1: happy path ---
status=SUBMITTED events=2

--- Scenario 2: idempotent consumer replay ---
processed evt-order-7001
skipped duplicate evt-order-7001

--- Scenario 3: failure — insufficient stock ---
rejected: insufficient stock: SKU-B
status=DRAFT events=0

--- Scenario 4: summary rules ---
Rule 1: modify one aggregate per transaction when possible
Rule 2: cross-context integration uses ports, not foreign entities
Rule 3: domain events are past-tense facts after state change
Rule 4: consumers must be idempotent under at-least-once delivery

sections.title: Microservices Principles & DDD — advanced
sections.description: Combined patterns: errors, pagination, contracts, or trade-off tables.
sections.output:
=== Block 1: boundary candidate scores ===
Formula: +2*autonomy +3*cohesion -2*coupling -2*slaConflict

billing score=33
catalog score=28
search-read score=-1

=== Block 2: example context map links ===
pricing -> orders : Customer-Supplier + PublishedLanguage
legacy-crm -> subscriptions : Anti-corruption Layer
identity -> all : Open Host Service (OIDC claims)

=== Block 3: decomposition decision reference ===
Symptom                              | Likely move
Shared DB + cross-team migrations    | Split data + events/API
Chatty sync chain p95 high             | Consolidate or async + saga
Different RPS on read vs write         | CQRS projection service
Team communication bottleneck          | Align Conway + contexts
Duplicate business rules in 2 services | Upstream published language
Unknown vocabulary drift               | Event storming + glossary
Legacy model leaking in               | ACL + translation DTOs
Duplicate side effects on retry        | Idempotency + outbox
Heavy join queries pre-split           | Read model + CDC/events
One team owns all                      | Modular monolith first

sections.title: Flow — bounded contexts, outbox, and async integration
sections.description: Shows one area saving its own database and writing an outbox row, then a message fan-out updating another area’s database without sharing tables.
sections.plantuml:
@startuml
title Day 51 — One area saves, outbox sends, another area catches up
actor Client
participant "Order API\n(one business area)" as Orders
participant "Inventory API\n(another area)" as Inv
database "Orders DB" as ODB
queue "Message Bus" as Bus
participant "Shipping\n(read/update)" as Ship
database "Shipping DB" as SDB

Client -> Orders : POST /orders
Orders -> Inv : reserve (sync call + timeout)
Inv --> Orders : OK
Orders -> ODB : save order + insert outbox row
Orders --> Client : 201 Created

Orders -> Bus : publisher sends OrderPlaced
Bus -> Ship : message may arrive more than once
Ship -> SDB : safe upsert (idempotent)

note right of Orders
  No shared JPA entities
  across areas
end note
note bottom of Bus
  Consumers must ignore
  duplicates safely
end note
@enduml

sections.title: Common pitfalls
items: Splitting by technical layer (only “database service”) instead of by business area — you still need every layer for each feature, but now with network calls in the middle.
Keeping one shared database “to go faster” — teams step on each other’s schema changes and true independent releases are almost impossible.
Treating a long chain of REST calls like one database transaction — partial failures leave half-finished business steps unless you design sagas, undo steps, and idempotency.
Sending events without an outbox — the row saves but the message never leaves, or the message sends twice, so other systems drift or duplicate work.
Using the same English word in two services for different meanings — tests look fine until finance reconciliation or customer support finds silent mismatches.
Exposing internal database ids and entities to other teams’ code — everyone couples to your schema, and simple refactors break other services.
Making one “aggregate” enormous so every small action locks too much data — slow responses and deadlocks show up under real load.
Ignoring traces and lag metrics for async flows — duplicate messages and stuck workflows look random until you add dashboards and alerts.

sections.title: Exercise — Checkout boundaries without one shared database
sections.problem:
You are breaking a monolith checkout into separate areas owned by different teams: **Orders**, **Inventory**, **Payments**, **Notifications**.

Write an architecture-first answer (prose is fine; code is optional).

1. Name **three business areas** (bounded contexts) and one **language risk** for each (same word, different meaning).
2. Say which steps should be **synchronous** (user waits) vs **async** (can finish shortly after), and give a **simple reason** for each choice.
3. Explain how the **order** keeps its own rules **without** calling another team’s JPA repository directly.
4. Describe **idempotency** for handling **PaymentCaptured** twice from the message bus.
5. Name **one metric or alert** you would use to catch **slow background steps** or **stuck workflows**.

Goal: show clear boundaries, not a perfect diagram.
hints: If totals must not change after submit, decide **one owner** for price/tax and store **frozen numbers** on the order.
Model checkout as **steps with states** (draft, submitted, paid) instead of one giant method.
Build dedup keys from **business ids** (order id + payment ref), not from Kafka offset numbers.

sections.title: Interview Drill

conceptual.question: What is a bounded context in DDD, and why is it more important than package naming?
conceptual.answer: A **bounded context** is a line around a part of the business where **words and rules stay consistent**. Package names alone do not stop teams from sharing one database or calling each other’s tables. In production, the real question is **who owns the data** and **who can deploy alone**. Interviewers want you to talk about **contracts** (APIs, events) and **ownership**, not folder trees.

followUps.question: Interview follow-up: how would you **verify** this contract in CI and production?
followUps.answer: For this topic, you cite OpenAPI diff gates, consumer contract tests (Pact), gateway access logs with trace IDs, and SLO dashboards (p95 latency, error rate by route). You separate what HTTP semantics guarantee from what your gateway and clients must implement.

followUps.question: What **trap** might the interviewer set next?
followUps.answer: They may conflate idempotency with safety, push on cache invalidation vs eventual consistency, or ask about breaking vs additive schema changes. You give precise status-code and retry semantics and name one operational metric you would watch.

conceptual.question: What is ubiquitous language, and how does it prevent production bugs?
conceptual.answer: **Ubiquitous language** means developers and business experts use the **same words** for the same ideas, in code, APIs, and UI. When words drift, two services can both use `total` but mean **different things** — local tests still pass, but month-end reports disagree. A small **glossary** and **contract tests** on important fields catch drift before customers do.

followUps.question: Interview follow-up: how would you **verify** this contract in CI and production?
followUps.answer: For this topic, you cite OpenAPI diff gates, consumer contract tests (Pact), gateway access logs with trace IDs, and SLO dashboards (p95 latency, error rate by route). You separate what HTTP semantics guarantee from what your gateway and clients must implement.

followUps.question: What **trap** might the interviewer set next?
followUps.answer: They may conflate idempotency with safety, push on cache invalidation vs eventual consistency, or ask about breaking vs additive schema changes. You give precise status-code and retry semantics and name one operational metric you would watch.

conceptual.question: What is an aggregate root, and why should other aggregates reference it by id?
conceptual.answer: The **aggregate root** is the **only supported way** to change a cluster of objects so **rules cannot be skipped**. Other aggregates should point with **ids** so you do not accidentally load giant graphs or pretend two services share one database transaction. In Spring, deep JPA links across areas often hide **coupling** — explicit ports and queries keep boundaries clear.

followUps.question: Interview follow-up: how would you **verify** this contract in CI and production?
followUps.answer: For this topic, you cite OpenAPI diff gates, consumer contract tests (Pact), gateway access logs with trace IDs, and SLO dashboards (p95 latency, error rate by route). You separate what HTTP semantics guarantee from what your gateway and clients must implement.

followUps.question: What **trap** might the interviewer set next?
followUps.answer: They may conflate idempotency with safety, push on cache invalidation vs eventual consistency, or ask about breaking vs additive schema changes. You give precise status-code and retry semantics and name one operational metric you would watch.

conceptual.question: What is a domain event, and when should you publish it versus calling another service synchronously?
conceptual.answer: A **domain event** records **something that already happened** (`OrderPlaced`). Use **messages** when the next step can happen **a moment later** and you want **looser coupling**. Use **sync HTTP** when the user or the next step **must know the answer now** — but always add **timeouts** and failure handling. To avoid “saved in DB but message lost,” many teams use a **transactional outbox** in the same database transaction as the save.

followUps.question: Interview follow-up: how would you **verify** this contract in CI and production?
followUps.answer: For this topic, you cite OpenAPI diff gates, consumer contract tests (Pact), gateway access logs with trace IDs, and SLO dashboards (p95 latency, error rate by route). You separate what HTTP semantics guarantee from what your gateway and clients must implement.

followUps.question: What **trap** might the interviewer set next?
followUps.answer: They may conflate idempotency with safety, push on cache invalidation vs eventual consistency, or ask about breaking vs additive schema changes. You give precise status-code and retry semantics and name one operational metric you would watch.

conceptual.question: Explain the anti-corruption layer (ACL) with a concrete integration example.
conceptual.answer: An **anti-corruption layer** is a **translator** at the edge of your system. Example: a legacy CRM sends `cust_status=GOLD`. Your app maps that to `LoyaltyTier.PREMIUM` with clear rules. Without a translator, foreign field names leak into your core code and **every vendor change** breaks your service. The ACL keeps your **core language** clean.

followUps.question: Interview follow-up: how would you **verify** this contract in CI and production?
followUps.answer: For this topic, you cite OpenAPI diff gates, consumer contract tests (Pact), gateway access logs with trace IDs, and SLO dashboards (p95 latency, error rate by route). You separate what HTTP semantics guarantee from what your gateway and clients must implement.

followUps.question: What **trap** might the interviewer set next?
followUps.answer: They may conflate idempotency with safety, push on cache invalidation vs eventual consistency, or ask about breaking vs additive schema changes. You give precise status-code and retry semantics and name one operational metric you would watch.

conceptual.question: What is a context map, and name two relationship types you would use in an interview.
conceptual.answer: A **context map** is a simple picture of **business areas** and **how they depend on each other**. Two common names: **Customer–Supplier** (downstream can ask upstream for changes) and **Anti-corruption layer** (downstream translates a foreign model). Saying “we call REST” is not enough — say **who leads the model** and **how translation happens**.

followUps.question: Interview follow-up: how would you **verify** this contract in CI and production?
followUps.answer: For this topic, you cite OpenAPI diff gates, consumer contract tests (Pact), gateway access logs with trace IDs, and SLO dashboards (p95 latency, error rate by route). You separate what HTTP semantics guarantee from what your gateway and clients must implement.

followUps.question: What **trap** might the interviewer set next?
followUps.answer: They may conflate idempotency with safety, push on cache invalidation vs eventual consistency, or ask about breaking vs additive schema changes. You give precise status-code and retry semantics and name one operational metric you would watch.

conceptual.question: How does DDD relate to microservices — mandatory, optional, or orthogonal?
conceptual.answer: They are **related but not the same**. **DDD** helps you **draw good boundaries** and **name things well**. **Microservices** are **how you deploy**. You can use DDD inside **one modular app** (often smart early). You can split microservices **without** DDD and get a messy distributed system. Strong answers pick microservices when **independence** is worth the **extra ops work**.

followUps.question: Interview follow-up: how would you **verify** this contract in CI and production?
followUps.answer: For this topic, you cite OpenAPI diff gates, consumer contract tests (Pact), gateway access logs with trace IDs, and SLO dashboards (p95 latency, error rate by route). You separate what HTTP semantics guarantee from what your gateway and clients must implement.

followUps.question: What **trap** might the interviewer set next?
followUps.answer: They may conflate idempotency with safety, push on cache invalidation vs eventual consistency, or ask about breaking vs additive schema changes. You give precise status-code and retry semantics and name one operational metric you would watch.

conceptual.question: What is the difference between an entity and a value object?
conceptual.answer: An **entity** has **identity** that stays the same even when fields change (`Customer`, `Order`). A **value object** is a **small immutable bundle** of data (`Money`, `Address`) compared by value. Mixing them causes bugs like sharing one `Money` object and mutating it from two places. Java **records** often fit value objects well.

followUps.question: Interview follow-up: how would you **verify** this contract in CI and production?
followUps.answer: For this topic, you cite OpenAPI diff gates, consumer contract tests (Pact), gateway access logs with trace IDs, and SLO dashboards (p95 latency, error rate by route). You separate what HTTP semantics guarantee from what your gateway and clients must implement.

followUps.question: What **trap** might the interviewer set next?
followUps.answer: They may conflate idempotency with safety, push on cache invalidation vs eventual consistency, or ask about breaking vs additive schema changes. You give precise status-code and retry semantics and name one operational metric you would watch.

conceptual.question: What is a repository in DDD, and what should not be inside it?
conceptual.answer: A **repository** **loads and saves aggregates** and hides SQL or JPA details. It should **not** own core business decisions like “should we refund?” — those belong in the **domain model**. If repositories grow huge if-else blocks, you likely slid back to a **transaction script** style.

followUps.question: Interview follow-up: how would you **verify** this contract in CI and production?
followUps.answer: For this topic, you cite OpenAPI diff gates, consumer contract tests (Pact), gateway access logs with trace IDs, and SLO dashboards (p95 latency, error rate by route). You separate what HTTP semantics guarantee from what your gateway and clients must implement.

followUps.question: What **trap** might the interviewer set next?
followUps.answer: They may conflate idempotency with safety, push on cache invalidation vs eventual consistency, or ask about breaking vs additive schema changes. You give precise status-code and retry semantics and name one operational metric you would watch.

conceptual.question: What is a domain service versus an application service?
conceptual.answer: A **domain service** holds a rule that does not fit one entity cleanly (for example, moving value between two accounts with joint rules). An **application service** is the **thin coordinator** for a use case: load aggregates, call domain code, save, publish integration messages. It should not become a **god class** with every rule inside.

followUps.question: Interview follow-up: how would you **verify** this contract in CI and production?
followUps.answer: For this topic, you cite OpenAPI diff gates, consumer contract tests (Pact), gateway access logs with trace IDs, and SLO dashboards (p95 latency, error rate by route). You separate what HTTP semantics guarantee from what your gateway and clients must implement.

followUps.question: What **trap** might the interviewer set next?
followUps.answer: They may conflate idempotency with safety, push on cache invalidation vs eventual consistency, or ask about breaking vs additive schema changes. You give precise status-code and retry semantics and name one operational metric you would watch.

conceptual.question: Why is 'anemic domain model' criticized, and when might it still be acceptable?
conceptual.answer: **Anemic domain model** means entities are mostly getters/setters and all logic sits in services — it often becomes hard-to-follow procedural code. For **simple CRUD** with almost no rules, that can be fine. For **money, risk, or compliance**, you usually want **richer domain objects** so rules stay in one obvious place.

followUps.question: Interview follow-up: how would you **verify** this contract in CI and production?
followUps.answer: For this topic, you cite OpenAPI diff gates, consumer contract tests (Pact), gateway access logs with trace IDs, and SLO dashboards (p95 latency, error rate by route). You separate what HTTP semantics guarantee from what your gateway and clients must implement.

followUps.question: What **trap** might the interviewer set next?
followUps.answer: They may conflate idempotency with safety, push on cache invalidation vs eventual consistency, or ask about breaking vs additive schema changes. You give precise status-code and retry semantics and name one operational metric you would watch.

conceptual.question: How do you handle cross-aggregate workflows without a distributed transaction?
conceptual.answer: Finish **one commit**, then **tell others with an event** (often via **outbox**). The next step runs in **another transaction**. Long flows use **sagas**: forward steps plus **undo steps** when something fails. In Java services, test that **duplicate messages** do not break the flow — because message systems usually deliver **at least once**.

followUps.question: Interview follow-up: how would you **verify** this contract in CI and production?
followUps.answer: For this topic, you cite OpenAPI diff gates, consumer contract tests (Pact), gateway access logs with trace IDs, and SLO dashboards (p95 latency, error rate by route). You separate what HTTP semantics guarantee from what your gateway and clients must implement.

followUps.question: What **trap** might the interviewer set next?
followUps.answer: They may conflate idempotency with safety, push on cache invalidation vs eventual consistency, or ask about breaking vs additive schema changes. You give precise status-code and retry semantics and name one operational metric you would watch.

conceptual.question: What is strategic design versus tactical design in DDD?
conceptual.answer: **Strategic** work answers **where the lines are** between business areas and teams (context map, workshops). **Tactical** work answers **how to code** inside one area (aggregates, events, repositories). Mixing them up gives either perfect diagrams with **messy code**, or pretty low-level code in the **wrong boundaries**.

followUps.question: Interview follow-up: how would you **verify** this contract in CI and production?
followUps.answer: For this topic, you cite OpenAPI diff gates, consumer contract tests (Pact), gateway access logs with trace IDs, and SLO dashboards (p95 latency, error rate by route). You separate what HTTP semantics guarantee from what your gateway and clients must implement.

followUps.question: What **trap** might the interviewer set next?
followUps.answer: They may conflate idempotency with safety, push on cache invalidation vs eventual consistency, or ask about breaking vs additive schema changes. You give precise status-code and retry semantics and name one operational metric you would watch.

conceptual.question: What is Conway’s Law, and how does it affect microservice decomposition?
conceptual.answer: **Conway’s Law** says software structure tends to match **how people communicate**. If communication is messy, boundaries will be messy too. Good splits align **service ownership** with **real teams**. Some teams also cite **Team Topologies** for platform vs stream-aligned teams — same idea in modern words.

followUps.question: Interview follow-up: how would you **verify** this contract in CI and production?
followUps.answer: For this topic, you cite OpenAPI diff gates, consumer contract tests (Pact), gateway access logs with trace IDs, and SLO dashboards (p95 latency, error rate by route). You separate what HTTP semantics guarantee from what your gateway and clients must implement.

followUps.question: What **trap** might the interviewer set next?
followUps.answer: They may conflate idempotency with safety, push on cache invalidation vs eventual consistency, or ask about breaking vs additive schema changes. You give precise status-code and retry semantics and name one operational metric you would watch.

conceptual.question: Where does CQRS fit relative to DDD and microservices?
conceptual.answer: **CQRS** means **separate models for reads and writes** when reads are heavy or very different from writes. DDD does **not** require it. In microservices, you often see **read databases** or **search indexes** filled from **events** or **CDC**. **Trade-off:** reads may be **slightly stale** — measure lag and agree with product what is acceptable.

followUps.question: Interview follow-up: how would you **verify** this contract in CI and production?
followUps.answer: For this topic, you cite OpenAPI diff gates, consumer contract tests (Pact), gateway access logs with trace IDs, and SLO dashboards (p95 latency, error rate by route). You separate what HTTP semantics guarantee from what your gateway and clients must implement.

followUps.question: What **trap** might the interviewer set next?
followUps.answer: They may conflate idempotency with safety, push on cache invalidation vs eventual consistency, or ask about breaking vs additive schema changes. You give precise status-code and retry semantics and name one operational metric you would watch.

conceptual.question: What is a **bounded context** in DDD, and why does it matter for microservices?
conceptual.answer: A bounded context is a **linguistic and model boundary** where terms have one consistent meaning. Aligning one service per context reduces ambiguous shared models. **Anti-pattern**: one giant “Customer” entity shared everywhere — contexts should exchange DTOs or events, not one shared table model.

followUps.question: Interview follow-up: how would you **verify** this contract in CI and production?

conceptual.question: When is **two services** worse than **one modular monolith**?
conceptual.answer: When teams, deployment cadence, and data ownership are not independent — distributed boundaries add **network, consistency, and ops** cost without autonomy benefits. Start modular inside one deployable unit; extract when **SLO, scale, or org** boundaries justify the split.

codeBased.question: How does **aggregate root** rule affect what you expose on a REST resource?
codeBased.answer: External updates should target the **aggregate root** URI; nested entities mutate **through** the root to enforce invariants. Exposing direct CRUD on every child table invites invariant violations. Document commands as sub-resources or domain actions when needed.

codeBased.question: Name a **domain event** you might publish when an order is **paid**, and one consumer.
codeBased.answer: Event: `OrderPaid` with order id, amount, timestamp. Consumer: **shipping** service reserves courier, **analytics** updates funnel metrics, **search** projection updates status. Keep payload minimal and versioned; avoid leaking internal entity graphs.

followUps.question: Interview follow-up: how would you **verify** this contract in CI and production?

followUps.question: What **trap** might the interviewer set next?
followUps.answer: They may conflate idempotency with safety, push on cache invalidation vs eventual consistency, or ask about breaking vs additive schema changes. You give precise status-code and retry semantics and name one operational metric you would watch.

seniorScenario.question: Teams split into microservices but still share one Postgres schema. Deployments are coupled and incidents require three on-call rotations. What do you do?
seniorScenario.answer: **(1) Immediate response:** Slow down changes that make coupling worse. Agree **one owner** for each table or start a **short freeze** on cross-team schema edits until there is a plan. **(2) Root cause:** Services are split on paper, but **one database** means **one big system** with network glue. Releases and rollbacks still touch the same tables. **(3) Fix:** Pick the **clearest business boundary** and move toward **separate data** step by step: stricter APIs, **read copies**, **events**, and an **anti-corruption** translator at the edge. Add **module rules** (ArchUnit) inside Java so illegal imports fail in CI. **(4) Prevention:** Write short **ADRs** per integration, add **contract tests**, and track metrics when two teams collide on migrations. The goal is **real ownership**, not more repos.

followUps.question: Interview follow-up: how would you **verify** this contract in CI and production?
followUps.answer: For this scenario, you cite OpenAPI diff gates, consumer contract tests (Pact), gateway access logs with trace IDs, and SLO dashboards (p95 latency, error rate by route). You separate what HTTP semantics guarantee from what your gateway and clients must implement.

followUps.question: What **trap** might the interviewer set next?
followUps.answer: They may conflate idempotency with safety, push on cache invalidation vs eventual consistency, or ask about breaking vs additive schema changes. You give precise status-code and retry semantics and name one operational metric you would watch.

seniorScenario.question: Finance reports mismatched totals after introducing a Pricing microservice. How do you investigate and resolve?
seniorScenario.answer: **(1) Immediate response:** Stop risky deploys, open a channel, and run a **reconciliation** between stored order amounts and invoice lines. **(2) Root cause:** Two areas both “own” **tax or rounding** with small differences. Each team tested alone, so the bug hid until real money moved. **(3) Fix:** Choose **one upstream owner** for pricing/tax rules, **version** the policy, and store **frozen line amounts** on submit. Add **consumer contract tests** on money fields. **(4) Prevention:** Keep a **glossary** for money words, dashboard **max daily drift**, and **feature flags** for policy changes. This is a **language and ownership** fix, not only “use BigDecimal.”

followUps.question: Interview follow-up: how would you **verify** this contract in CI and production?
followUps.answer: For this scenario, you cite OpenAPI diff gates, consumer contract tests (Pact), gateway access logs with trace IDs, and SLO dashboards (p95 latency, error rate by route). You separate what HTTP semantics guarantee from what your gateway and clients must implement.

followUps.question: What **trap** might the interviewer set next?
followUps.answer: They may conflate idempotency with safety, push on cache invalidation vs eventual consistency, or ask about breaking vs additive schema changes. You give precise status-code and retry semantics and name one operational metric you would watch.

seniorScenario.question: Kafka consumers sometimes double-credit loyalty points on redelivery. Your handler logs look idempotent — but duplicates persist. What happened?
seniorScenario.answer: **(1) Immediate response:** If money is involved, **pause payouts** or use a flag, then fix dedup before large replays. **(2) Root cause:** Kafka may deliver **more than once**. Idempotency that lives **only in memory**, or **ack before the database commit**, means a retry can **run the business logic twice**. **(3) Fix:** Use a **stable business key** in a **database unique constraint**, update ledger and mark processed in **one transaction**, then ack. Prefer **outbox** from the writer service so publishing matches the DB commit. **(4) Prevention:** Chaos-style tests with **forced duplicates**, metrics counting **ignored duplicates**, and alerts on **dead-letter** growth. Model the rule **credit once per order** inside the domain, not only in logs.

followUps.question: Interview follow-up: how would you **verify** this contract in CI and production?
followUps.answer: For this scenario, you cite OpenAPI diff gates, consumer contract tests (Pact), gateway access logs with trace IDs, and SLO dashboards (p95 latency, error rate by route). You separate what HTTP semantics guarantee from what your gateway and clients must implement.

followUps.question: What **trap** might the interviewer set next?
followUps.answer: They may conflate idempotency with safety, push on cache invalidation vs eventual consistency, or ask about breaking vs additive schema changes. You give precise status-code and retry semantics and name one operational metric you would watch.

seniorScenario.question: Product demands a synchronous 12-service chain to render a dashboard. Latency SLO is missed weekly. Your proposal?
seniorScenario.answer: **(1) Immediate response:** Add **timeouts** and **resource limits** so one slow service cannot stall everything; cache stable pieces. **(2) Root cause:** The dashboard is really a **read problem**, but it was built as a **live chain of writes services**. Boundaries were ignored for speed of feature work. **(3) Fix:** Build a **read model** (materialized view or search index) updated by **events** or **CDC**, optionally behind a **BFF** for the UI. Accept **small staleness** and show it honestly. **(4) Prevention:** **SLO per hop**, design review for deep chains, and platform help for **standard projection** patterns. CQRS here is a **tool**, not a religion — use it where reads hurt.

followUps.question: Interview follow-up: how would you **verify** this contract in CI and production?
followUps.answer: For this scenario, you cite OpenAPI diff gates, consumer contract tests (Pact), gateway access logs with trace IDs, and SLO dashboards (p95 latency, error rate by route). You separate what HTTP semantics guarantee from what your gateway and clients must implement.

followUps.question: What **trap** might the interviewer set next?
followUps.answer: They may conflate idempotency with safety, push on cache invalidation vs eventual consistency, or ask about breaking vs additive schema changes. You give precise status-code and retry semantics and name one operational metric you would watch.

seniorScenario.question: A startup with 6 engineers wants microservices 'for scalability.' How do you steer the conversation?
seniorScenario.answer: **(1) Immediate response:** Agree on the **real goal** (speed, hiring, compliance) without assuming microservices are the only path. **(2) Root cause:** Microservices add **deploy pipelines, monitoring, and failure modes** early. A tiny team often pays that cost before they get product clarity. **(3) Fix:** Use **one app with clear modules** and **strict import rules**, measure traffic and pain, then extract **one** service when data proves it (scale, compliance, or release conflict). **(4) Prevention:** Simple **decision records** before new services, and **templates** for tracing and auth when services appear. Pragmatic DDD beats **resume-driven** splitting.

followUps.question: Interview follow-up: how would you **verify** this contract in CI and production?
followUps.answer: For this scenario, you cite OpenAPI diff gates, consumer contract tests (Pact), gateway access logs with trace IDs, and SLO dashboards (p95 latency, error rate by route). You separate what HTTP semantics guarantee from what your gateway and clients must implement.

followUps.question: What **trap** might the interviewer set next?
followUps.answer: They may conflate idempotency with safety, push on cache invalidation vs eventual consistency, or ask about breaking vs additive schema changes. You give precise status-code and retry semantics and name one operational metric you would watch.

seniorScenario.question: Legacy ERP forces ugly data shapes into your clean domain. Teams start copying ERP columns into core entities. How do you stop the bleed?
seniorScenario.answer: **(1) Immediate response:** Stop new **ERP field copy-paste** into core models; route changes through a **translator** layer. **(2) Root cause:** Shortcuts feel faster until every ERP upgrade **breaks production** because your core model is secretly their model. **(3) Fix:** A focused **integration module or service** maps ERP rows to **your types**, with tests and versioned mappings; use **flags** for risky mapping changes. **(4) Prevention:** Mark ERP as **upstream** on the context map, test adapters in CI, and never use ERP tables as your core **`@Entity`** graph. Keep **your words** inside, **their words** outside.

followUps.question: Interview follow-up: how would you **verify** this contract in CI and production?
followUps.answer: For this scenario, you cite OpenAPI diff gates, consumer contract tests (Pact), gateway access logs with trace IDs, and SLO dashboards (p95 latency, error rate by route). You separate what HTTP semantics guarantee from what your gateway and clients must implement.

followUps.question: What **trap** might the interviewer set next?
followUps.answer: They may conflate idempotency with safety, push on cache invalidation vs eventual consistency, or ask about breaking vs additive schema changes. You give precise status-code and retry semantics and name one operational metric you would watch.

wrongAnswers: DDD requires microservices — Wrong: DDD is about modeling; many teams use it inside one modular application.
One microservice per database table — Wrong: that creates tiny chatty services; split by business area and team ownership instead.
Make aggregates as big as possible for strong consistency — Wrong: huge aggregates hurt performance; cross-area consistency is usually eventual with events.
Domain events must use Kafka — Wrong: events are an idea; delivery can be outbox, queue, log, or even sync while you are small.
Repositories should hold core business rules — Wrong: rules belong in the domain model; repositories should mostly load and save.
Anti-corruption layer means blocking external data — Wrong: it **translates** data into your model; it still accepts valid input.
Retry every REST call blindly — Wrong: retries on non-idempotent work duplicate side effects; use keys, sagas, and clear policies.
Same database but separate deploys equals microservices — Wrong: shared schema usually means a distributed monolith with extra pain.

sections.title: MCQ Quiz — Microservices Principles & DDD
sections.description: Thirty questions from basic to advanced — Microservices Principles & DDD. Read every option; distractors are plausible but wrong for a precise reason.

questions.question: [Microservices & DDD] A **bounded context** defines:

options.A: A model boundary with one ubiquitous language

questions.explanation: Terms mean one thing inside the context.

questions.question: [Microservices & DDD] An **aggregate root** enforces:

options.A: Invariants for a cluster of entities

questions.explanation: External updates go through the root.

options.A: Something that happened in the domain

options.C: Only DB column names without code

options.A: Entity has identity; VO is defined by attributes

questions.question: [Microservices & DDD] **Repository** abstracts:
questions.explanation: Domain speaks to persistence through repository.

questions.question: [Microservices & DDD] Splitting services by **team boundary** follows:
[Microservices & DDD] **Conformist** pattern accepts:
questions.explanation: When influence is low; avoid fighting their model.

questions.question: [Microservices & DDD] **ACL** translation belongs at:
questions.explanation: Contain foreign concepts at the edge.

options.A: A rule that must always hold for the model

questions.question: [Microservices & DDD] **Event storming** is a workshop technique to:

options.A: Entities are data bags; logic elsewhere
A generic API for many consumers
Prefer service per context when autonomy warrants

sections.title: Microservices Principles & DDD — cheatsheet
sections.content:
| Topic | Rule of thumb | Interview one-liner |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Bounded context | One area, one meaning per word | Draw lines around language and data ownership |
| Aggregate | One door for changes; one transaction focus | Keep commits small; use events across areas |
| Ubiquitous language | Same words in code and meetings | Drift shows up as money and support bugs |
| Context map | Who leads; who translates | Name relationships, not only HTTP routes |
| Microservices | Split when independence is worth cost | Buy team speed; pay ops and network tax |
| Modular monolith | Modules first, services later | Prove boundaries before splitting deploys |
| Domain event | Past fact after a change | Outbox keeps DB and messages aligned |
| ACL | Translator at the edge | Keep vendor shapes out of core entities |
| CQRS | Split read and write when reads hurt | Accept small staleness; measure lag |
| Saga | Multi-step flow with undo steps | Replace fantasy distributed transactions |
| Trace id | Propagate on every hop | Debug 502/504 with correlation |
| Timeouts | Client < gateway < server | Cancel work; avoid retry storms |
| Payload size | Cap upload; stream large | Protect memory and threads |
| Deprecation | Sunset header + docs | Give consumers a calendar |
| Security | Authn at edge; authz in service | Do not trust gateway alone |
